1:
"And after these things I saw four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth, that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree." The witnesses note the four angels restraining the four winds. Victorinus notes (in his reading) the four angels and the angel with the seal point to the restraining of judgment until the servants of God are sealed and established against the great persecution. The witnesses note John seeing four angels at the four corners of the earth holding the four winds, that no wind should blow on earth, sea, or tree; the restraining of judgment until God's servants are sealed — that, between the sixth and seventh seals, John sees a pause and a sealing: "four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth, that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree"; the witnesses note the figure — the "four winds," held back by the angels, are the destroying judgments of God (war, calamity, desolation) ready to be loosed upon the world; the four angels "holding" them signify that these judgments are under God's restraint, kept back for a time so that no harm comes to "earth," "sea," or "tree" until a needful work is done; so that God, in his providence, holds back the storm of judgment until his own people are made safe; that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree. The witnesses note four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth. The witnesses note that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree.

The witnesses note the four angels restraining the four winds — the destroying judgments of God held back under his restraint, so that no harm comes until his people are sealed and made safe.

So the witnesses make the verse the restraining of judgment until the sealing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the destroying winds of judgment are held back under God's restraint until his servants are sealed. The witnesses note the four angels: "four angels standing on the four corners of the earth" — at the corners of the earth; the four angels; the the corners; the the earth; the standing; the the winds. The expositors observe the holding the winds: "holding the four winds of the earth" — restraining the winds; the holding the winds; the restraining; the the four winds; the held back; the the judgments. The witnesses press the the winds are judgments: the winds are the destroying judgments of God; the the winds are judgments; the destroying; the war; the desolation; the loosed. The expositors draw the lesson on the under restraint: under God's restraint, kept back for a time; the under restraint; the God's; the kept back; the for a time; the held. The witnesses observe the no harm: "that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree" — no harm comes; the no harm; the the earth; the the sea; the the tree; the spared. The expositors note the holds the storm: God holds back the storm until his people are safe; the holds the storm; the his people; the the sealing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that the wind should not blow on the earth nor on the sea nor on any tree, and the restraining of judgment. The verse thus records the restraining of judgment until the sealing — teaching that the destroying winds are held back under God's restraint until his servants are sealed.
2:
"And I saw another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God: and he cried with a loud voice to the four angels, to whom it was given to hurt the earth and the sea," The witnesses note the angel from the east with the seal of the living God. Victorinus notes (in his reading) the angel ascending from the east with the seal is the herald of restoration, sealing and establishing the churches before the loosing of judgment. The witnesses note John seeing another angel ascend from the east with the seal of the living God, crying to the four angels who were to hurt the earth and sea; the angel with God's seal staying the judgment to mark out God's own — that John sees a further messenger: "another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God"; the witnesses note the two marks of this angel and his errand — he comes "from the east" (the quarter of light and of God's blessing) bearing "the seal of the living God" (the instrument by which God's people are to be marked as his own) — and "he cried with a loud voice to the four angels, to whom it was given to hurt the earth and the sea," charging them to stay their destroying work; the witnesses note the gracious order of God's providence — before the judgments are loosed, the sealing angel goes forth to mark out and secure God's people, that they may be known and kept; so that God ever cares for his own before he sends the storm; another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God. The witnesses note another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God. The witnesses note he cried with a loud voice to the four angels, to whom it was given to hurt the earth and the sea.

The witnesses note the angel from the east with the seal of the living God, charging the four angels to stay their judgment — God marking out his own before the storm is loosed.

So the witnesses make the verse the sealing angel staying the judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that, before the judgments are loosed, an angel with God's seal marks out and secures his people. The witnesses note the another angel: "another angel ascending from the east" — from the quarter of light; the another angel; the from the east; the the light; the God's blessing; the the herald. The expositors observe the seal of God: "having the seal of the living God" — the instrument to mark God's people; the seal of God; the the instrument; the mark; the his own; the the living God. The witnesses press the cried to the four: "he cried with a loud voice to the four angels" — charging them to stay; the cried to the four; the charging; the stay; the the four angels; the the judgment. The expositors draw the lesson on the to hurt: "to whom it was given to hurt the earth and the sea" — who were to hurt the earth and sea; the to hurt; the the judgment; the stayed. The witnesses observe the seals first: before judgment, the sealing angel goes forth; the seals first; the before judgment; the the sealing; the goes forth; the marks. The expositors note the cares for his own: God cares for his own before the storm; the cares for his own; the secured; the kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he cried with a loud voice to the four angels, and the sealing angel. The verse thus records the sealing angel staying the judgment — teaching that, before the judgments are loosed, God marks out and secures his people.
3:
"Saying, Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads." The witnesses note the charge to stay the judgment until God's servants are sealed. Gill notes (in the historicist reading) the commission to hurt was not yet to be carried out, but at the appointed time, at the blowing of the trumpets, the winds were let loose; the sealing comes first. The witnesses note John hearing the angel say, "Hurt not the earth, the sea, or the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads"; the staying of all harm until God's servants are marked on the forehead — that the sealing angel's word is recorded: "Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads"; the witnesses note the purpose and the manner of the sealing — the judgments are held back until "the servants of our God" are "sealed," marked out as his own and secured against the coming harm, and that "in their foreheads" (an open, visible mark of ownership, as the Spirit seals believers and sets God's mark upon them); the witnesses note the comfort — God will not let the storm fall until every one of his own is sealed and safe; so that the believer is divinely marked and kept, distinguished and preserved amid the judgments that fall on the world; till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads. The witnesses note Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees. The witnesses note till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads.

The witnesses note the charge to stay all harm until God's servants are sealed in their foreheads — marked as his own and secured; the storm not falling until every one of his own is safe.

So the witnesses make the verse the sealing of God's servants before judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the judgments are held back until God's servants are marked and secured as his own. The witnesses note the hurt not: "Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees" — Gill notes the harm held back; the hurt not; the held back; the the earth; the the sea; the the trees. The expositors observe the till sealed: "till we have sealed the servants of our God" — until God's servants are sealed; the till sealed; the the servants; the of God; the marked; the secured. The witnesses press the in their foreheads: "in their foreheads" — an open, visible mark of ownership; the in their foreheads; the open; the visible; the mark; the ownership. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Spirit seals: as the Spirit seals believers and sets God's mark; the the Spirit seals; the believers; the God's mark; the sets; the marked. The witnesses observe the held back until safe: the storm held back until his own are safe; the held back until safe; the the storm; the his own; the safe; the sealed. The expositors note the marked and kept: the believer is divinely marked and kept; the marked and kept; the divinely; the distinguished; the preserved; the amid judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Hurt not the earth neither the sea nor the trees, the till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads, and the sealing of God's servants. The verse thus records the sealing of God's servants before judgment — teaching that the judgments are held back until God's servants are marked and secured.
4:
"And I heard the number of them which were sealed: and there were sealed an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the number of the sealed — 144,000 of the tribes of Israel. Gill notes John heard the number, and so could be sure of the exact count, which did not depend on his sight and telling, in which some mistake might be made. The witnesses note John hearing that the number sealed was a hundred and forty-four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel; the definite, complete number of the sealed drawn from Israel's tribes — that John, having seen the sealing, now "heard the number of them which were sealed: and there were sealed an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel"; the witnesses note the significance of the number — 144,000 (twelve thousand from each of twelve tribes) is a definite, ordered, and complete number, marking that God knows and secures the exact tale of his own, not one missing; the witnesses note the debate on "the tribes of the children of Israel" — some take it of literal, ethnic Israel, a remnant of the Jews sealed and preserved; others of the spiritual Israel, the whole church of God under the figure of the twelve tribes, the symbolic number standing for the full and perfect number of the elect; either way, the sealed are a complete and known company, kept by God; there were sealed an hundred and forty and four thousand. The witnesses note I heard the number of them which were sealed. The witnesses note an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel.

The witnesses note the number sealed — 144,000 of the tribes of Israel — a definite, complete number, with debate whether it means literal ethnic Israel or the whole spiritual Israel, the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the complete number of the sealed. The lesson the expositors draw is that God knows and secures the exact, complete number of his own, none missing. The witnesses note the heard the number: "I heard the number of them which were sealed" — Gill notes he heard it, so was sure of the count; the heard the number; the heard; the sure; the the count; the exact. The expositors observe the 144,000: "an hundred and forty and four thousand" — twelve thousand from each tribe; the 144,000; the twelve thousand; the each tribe; the definite; the complete. The witnesses press the ordered and complete: a definite, ordered, complete number; the ordered and complete; the definite; the ordered; the complete; the the tale. The witnesses present the debate: the witnesses note the debate on "the tribes of Israel" — some take it of literal ethnic Israel, a remnant of the Jews; others of the spiritual Israel, the whole church under the figure of the tribes. The expositors draw the lesson on the not one missing: God knows the exact tale, not one missing; the not one missing; the the exact tale; the his own; the secured; the known. The witnesses observe the complete company: the sealed are a complete, known company; the complete company; the known; the kept; the by God; the secured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I heard the number of them which were sealed, the an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel, and the complete number. The verse thus records the complete number of the sealed — teaching that God knows and secures the exact, complete number of his own.
5:
"Of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Reuben were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand." The witnesses note the sealing of twelve thousand from each of the first tribes named, beginning with Judah. Gill notes Judah is mentioned first because Christ sprang from that tribe, and the pure worship of God was preserved in it; its name signifies "praise." The witnesses note John recording twelve thousand sealed of Judah, of Reuben, and of Gad; the equal sealing of each tribe, Judah first for Christ's sake — that John begins the roll of the sealed tribe by tribe: "of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand," and so of Reuben and Gad; the witnesses note two things — first, the equal number, "twelve thousand" from each tribe, marking that none is favoured or neglected, each having its full and equal share among the sealed; and second, the order, that "Juda" is named first, not by natural birthright (for Reuben was the firstborn), but because of Christ, who "sprang out of Juda," and because the worship of God was preserved in it; the witnesses note that Judah's name signifies "praise," a fitting head for the company of the sealed who are gathered to praise God; so that the sealing is orderly, equal, and headed by the tribe of the Messiah; of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Reuben... of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand.

The witnesses note twelve thousand sealed of each tribe — equal and full — Judah named first not by birthright but for Christ who sprang from it; the sealing orderly and headed by the Messiah's tribe.

So the witnesses make the verse the equal sealing of the tribes, Judah first. The lesson the expositors draw is that each tribe has its full, equal share among the sealed, Judah named first for Christ's sake. The witnesses note the twelve thousand each: "of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand" — equal number from each tribe; the twelve thousand each; the equal; the each tribe; the full share; the none neglected. The expositors observe the none favoured: none favoured or neglected; the none favoured; the none neglected; the equal share; the each; the full. The witnesses press the Judah first: Judah named first, not by birthright; the Judah first; the not birthright; the Reuben firstborn; the named first; the Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ sprang from it: because Christ sprang out of Judah; the Christ sprang from it; the Christ; the out of Judah; the the Messiah; the the tribe. The witnesses observe the worship preserved: and the worship of God preserved in it; the worship preserved; the God's worship; the in Judah; the pure; the kept. The expositors note the praise: Judah's name signifies "praise," fitting for the sealed; the praise; the the name; the signifies; the fitting; the the sealed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand, the of the tribe of Reuben and of Gad were sealed twelve thousand, and the equal sealing. The verse thus records the equal sealing of the tribes, Judah first — teaching that each tribe has its full, equal share, Judah named first for Christ's sake.
6:
"Of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Nepthalim were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Manasses were sealed twelve thousand." The witnesses note the continuing roll of the sealed tribes — Asher, Naphtali, and Manasses. Gill notes Asher, which the Septuagint pronounce "Aser," signifies "blessed," as all the sealed ones are, blessed with all spiritual blessings, with grace here and glory hereafter. The witnesses note John recording twelve thousand sealed of Asher, of Naphtali, and of Manasses; the roll of the sealed continued, each tribe's name bearing a fitting sense — that John continues the equal sealing through the next tribes: "of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand," and so of Naphtali and Manasses; the witnesses note that the commentators (Gill especially) observe a fitness in the very names — "Asher" signifies "blessed," fitting for the sealed who are blessed with all spiritual blessings, grace now and glory to come; the witnesses note the steady, equal continuance of the count, twelve thousand from each, the full number gathered from every tribe; so that the sealing proceeds without favour or omission, each tribe yielding its appointed company of the redeemed, the names themselves whispering the blessedness of those whom God has sealed; of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Nepthalim... of the tribe of Manasses were sealed twelve thousand.

The witnesses note the roll continued — Asher, Naphtali, Manasses — each twelve thousand; the names themselves fitting, "Asher" meaning "blessed," whispering the blessedness of the sealed.

So the witnesses make the verse the continued equal sealing of the tribes. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sealing proceeds without favour, each tribe yielding its full company, the names fitting the blessedness of the sealed. The witnesses note the Asher sealed: "of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand" — Gill notes Asher signifies "blessed"; the Asher sealed; the Asher; the blessed; the the name; the fitting. The expositors observe the blessed: the sealed are blessed with all spiritual blessings; the blessed; the spiritual blessings; the grace; the glory; the the sealed. The witnesses press the Naphtali and Manasses: "of the tribe of Nepthalim... of Manasses" — the roll continued; the Naphtali and Manasses; the continued; the the roll; the each tribe; the twelve thousand. The expositors draw the lesson on the equal count: the equal count, twelve thousand each; the equal count; the twelve thousand; the each; the full; the steady. The witnesses observe the no omission: without favour or omission; the no omission; the no favour; the each tribe; the appointed; the full. The expositors note the names whisper: the names themselves whisper the blessedness of the sealed; the names whisper; the the names; the blessedness; the the sealed; the fitting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand, the of the tribe of Nepthalim and of Manasses were sealed twelve thousand, and the continued sealing. The verse thus records the continued equal sealing of the tribes — teaching that the sealing proceeds without favour, each tribe yielding its full company.
7:
"Of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Levi were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand." The witnesses note the further roll — Simeon, Levi, and Issachar — each twelve thousand sealed. Gill notes Simeon signifies "hearing," and such a hearing as is attended with obedience, denoting the spiritual and practical hearing of the gospel by those who hear, understand, believe, and practise. The witnesses note John recording twelve thousand sealed of Simeon, of Levi, and of Issachar; the roll continued, the names again whispering the graces of the sealed — that John continues: "of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand," and so of Levi and Issachar; the witnesses note again the fitness the commentators find in the names — "Simeon" signifies "hearing," the spiritual, obedient hearing of the gospel that marks the truly sealed, who hear, understand, believe, and obey; the witnesses note that even Levi, the priestly tribe, has its twelve thousand, none excluded from the sealed company; the steady equal count goes on, each tribe yielding its full number; so that the sealing of God's people is complete, orderly, and gathered from every quarter, the very names of the tribes adorned with the graces of those whom God seals — hearing, joining, and the rest; of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Levi... of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand.

The witnesses note the roll continued — Simeon, Levi, Issachar — each twelve thousand; the names again fitting, "Simeon" meaning "hearing," the obedient hearing of the gospel that marks the sealed.

So the witnesses make the verse the continued sealing through Simeon, Levi, Issachar. The lesson the expositors draw is that each tribe yields its full number, the names fitting the graces of the sealed. The witnesses note the Simeon sealed: "of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand" — Gill notes Simeon signifies "hearing"; the Simeon sealed; the Simeon; the hearing; the the name; the fitting. The expositors observe the obedient hearing: the spiritual, obedient hearing of the gospel; the obedient hearing; the spiritual; the the gospel; the believe; the obey. The witnesses press the Levi included: "of the tribe of Levi" — even the priestly tribe has its number; the Levi included; the the priestly; the its number; the none excluded; the sealed. The expositors draw the lesson on the Issachar: "of the tribe of Issachar" — the roll continued; the Issachar; the continued; the the roll; the twelve thousand; the each. The witnesses observe the steady count: the steady equal count goes on; the steady count; the equal; the each tribe; the full; the goes on. The expositors note the complete and orderly: the sealing is complete, orderly, from every quarter; the complete and orderly; the complete; the orderly; the every quarter; the gathered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand, the of the tribe of Levi and of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand, and the continued sealing. The verse thus records the continued sealing through Simeon, Levi, Issachar — teaching that each tribe yields its full number, the names fitting the graces of the sealed.
8:
"Of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Joseph were sealed twelve thousand. Of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand." The witnesses note the closing of the roll — Zebulun, Joseph, and Benjamin — completing the 144,000. Gill notes Zebulun signifies "dwelling," and was the tribe in which Christ had his dwelling and much conversed, denoting his gracious inhabitation among the saints and sealed. The witnesses note John recording twelve thousand sealed of Zebulun, of Joseph, and of Benjamin, completing the number; the roll of the sealed brought to its full and complete sum — that John closes the list: "of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand," and so of Joseph and Benjamin, completing the twelve tribes and the full 144,000; the witnesses note once more the fitness in the names — "Zebulun" signifies "dwelling," the tribe where Christ much dwelt, fitting for the sealed in whom Christ and the Father and the Spirit graciously dwell; the witnesses note the completion of the sealing — every tribe has now yielded its twelve thousand, the whole company of the sealed gathered and sure, not one tribe omitted, not one of the appointed number lacking; so that the sealing of God's people is full, complete, and final, every one of his own marked and kept before the judgments fall; of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand. The witnesses note of the tribe of Joseph... of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand.

The witnesses note the roll closed — Zebulun, Joseph, Benjamin — completing the 144,000; the sealing full and final, every tribe yielding its number, every one of God's own marked and kept.

So the witnesses make the verse the completion of the sealing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sealing is brought to its full sum, every tribe yielding its number, the whole company sealed and sure. The witnesses note the Zebulun sealed: "of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand" — Gill notes Zebulun signifies "dwelling"; the Zebulun sealed; the Zebulun; the dwelling; the the name; the fitting. The expositors observe the Christ dwelt: the tribe where Christ much dwelt; the Christ dwelt; the the tribe; the much dwelt; the gracious inhabitation; the the sealed. The witnesses press the Joseph and Benjamin: "of the tribe of Joseph... of Benjamin" — the roll closed; the Joseph and Benjamin; the closed; the the roll; the completing; the twelve thousand. The expositors draw the lesson on the full sum: completing the twelve tribes and the full 144,000; the full sum; the the twelve tribes; the the 144,000; the complete; the full. The witnesses observe the not one lacking: not one tribe omitted, not one number lacking; the not one lacking; the no tribe omitted; the the appointed; the complete; the full. The expositors note the full and final: the sealing is full, complete, and final; the full and final; the complete; the final; the every one; the marked. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand, the of the tribe of Joseph and of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand, and the completed sealing. The verse thus records the completion of the sealing — teaching that the sealing is brought to its full sum, the whole company sealed and sure.
9:
"After this I beheld, and, lo, a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues, stood before the throne, and before the Lamb, clothed with white robes, and palms in their hands;" The witnesses note the innumerable multitude of all nations before the throne. Gill notes this is a distinct vision from the preceding; here is no mention of sealing or numbering, for this great multitude is gathered out of all nations, an innumerable company. The witnesses note John beholding a great multitude no man could number, of all nations, before the throne and the Lamb, in white robes with palms; the countless redeemed of every nation standing in triumph before the throne — that John, after the sealing of the numbered tribes, sees a wider and more glorious vision: "a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues, stood before the throne, and before the Lamb"; the witnesses note the breadth and the glory of this company — innumerable (beyond all counting), drawn "of all nations" (not Israel only but the redeemed of every people, kindred, and tongue), standing "before the throne, and before the Lamb" (in God's immediate presence), "clothed with white robes" (the robes of purity, victory, and the righteousness of Christ) "and palms in their hands" (the palms of victory and festal joy); so that the sealed are seen at last triumphant and glorified, a vast multitude of every nation gathered safe before God and the Lamb; a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations. The witnesses note a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues. The witnesses note clothed with white robes, and palms in their hands.

The witnesses note the innumerable multitude of all nations before the throne — the redeemed of every people, in white robes with palms; the sealed seen at last triumphant and glorified.

So the witnesses make the verse the countless redeemed of every nation before the throne. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sealed are seen triumphant, a vast multitude of every nation in white robes with palms before God and the Lamb. The witnesses note the great multitude: "a great multitude, which no man could number" — Gill notes an innumerable company; the great multitude; the innumerable; the no man could number; the countless; the vast. The expositors observe the all nations: "of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues" — of every people; the all nations; the every people; the kindreds; the tongues; the not Israel only. The witnesses press the before the throne: "stood before the throne, and before the Lamb" — in God's immediate presence; the before the throne; the the Lamb; the God's presence; the standing; the gathered. The expositors draw the lesson on the white robes: "clothed with white robes" — purity, victory, Christ's righteousness; the white robes; the purity; the victory; the righteousness; the clothed. The witnesses observe the palms: "and palms in their hands" — the palms of victory and joy; the palms; the victory; the festal joy; the in their hands; the triumph. The expositors note the triumphant: the sealed seen at last triumphant and glorified; the triumphant; the glorified; the the sealed; the safe; the gathered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a great multitude which no man could number of all nations and kindreds and people and tongues, the clothed with white robes and palms in their hands, and the countless redeemed. The verse thus records the countless redeemed of every nation before the throne — teaching that the sealed are seen triumphant, a vast multitude of every nation before God and the Lamb.
10:
"And cried with a loud voice, saying, Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb." The witnesses note the multitude's loud ascription of salvation to God and the Lamb. Gill notes they cried with a loud voice, to show the strength of their affection, the greatness of their joy, and how sensible they were of the favour they enjoyed, ascribing the glory of salvation to God and the Lamb. The witnesses note John hearing the multitude cry with a loud voice, "Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb"; the redeemed ascribing their whole salvation to God and the Lamb — that the great multitude lifts a triumphant song: "Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb"; the witnesses note the substance of their praise — they ascribe "salvation" wholly to God and to the Lamb, owning that their deliverance and glory are not their own work but the gift and achievement of "our God" (the Father on the throne) and "the Lamb" (Christ who redeemed them by his blood); the witnesses note the loudness and strength of the cry, the measure of their joy and gratitude; so that the redeemed in glory give all the praise of their salvation to the Father and the Son, claiming nothing for themselves, but crowning God and the Lamb with the glory of saving them; Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb. The witnesses note cried with a loud voice, Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne. The witnesses note and unto the Lamb.

The witnesses note the redeemed ascribing salvation wholly to God and the Lamb — owning their deliverance not their own work but the gift of the Father on the throne and the Lamb who redeemed them.

So the witnesses make the verse the ascription of salvation to God and the Lamb. The lesson the expositors draw is that the redeemed give all the praise of their salvation to the Father and the Son, claiming nothing for themselves. The witnesses note the loud voice: "cried with a loud voice" — Gill notes the strength of their affection and joy; the loud voice; the strength; the affection; the joy; the gratitude. The expositors observe the salvation: "Salvation to our God" — they ascribe salvation wholly to God; the salvation; the to God; the wholly; the the deliverance; the his gift. The witnesses press the on the throne: "which sitteth upon the throne" — the Father on the throne; the on the throne; the the Father; the the throne; the God; the sits. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Lamb: "and unto the Lamb" — and to Christ who redeemed them; the the Lamb; the Christ; the redeemed; the by his blood; the the Son. The witnesses observe the not their own work: their deliverance not their own work but God's gift; the not their own work; the God's gift; the the achievement; the not their own; the the Lamb's. The expositors note the all the praise: they give all the praise to the Father and Son; the all the praise; the the Father; the the Son; the nothing for themselves; the crown. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the cried with a loud voice Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, the and unto the Lamb, and the ascription of salvation. The verse thus records the ascription of salvation to God and the Lamb — teaching that the redeemed give all the praise of their salvation to the Father and the Son.
11:
"And all the angels stood round about the throne, and about the elders and the four beasts, and fell before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God," The witnesses note the angels joining the worship before the throne. Gill notes all the angels, the holy and elect angels, the ten thousand times ten thousand, the innumerable company, stood round about the throne, the elders, and the four living creatures. The witnesses note John seeing all the angels stand about the throne, the elders, and the four beasts, and fall on their faces and worship God; the angelic host joining the redeemed in worship before the throne — that John sees the heavenly worship widen to include the angels: "all the angels stood round about the throne, and about the elders and the four beasts, and fell before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God"; the witnesses note the order and the reverence — the innumerable host of angels, encircling the throne, the elders (representing the redeemed church), and the four living creatures, join the worship, "falling on their faces" in the deepest reverence and adoration; the witnesses note that, though the angels have no part in the salvation the redeemed celebrate, they yet rejoice in it and worship the God who saves; so that heaven's whole company — redeemed and angels alike — bows before the throne in united adoration of God; all the angels stood round about the throne... and worshipped God. The witnesses note all the angels stood round about the throne, and about the elders and the four beasts. The witnesses note fell before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God.

The witnesses note the angelic host joining the worship — falling on their faces before the throne; the angels, though having no part in salvation, rejoicing in it and worshipping the God who saves.

So the witnesses make the verse the angels joining the heavenly worship. The lesson the expositors draw is that the innumerable angels join the redeemed in falling before the throne to worship God. The witnesses note the all the angels: "all the angels stood round about the throne" — Gill notes the innumerable host; the all the angels; the the host; the innumerable; the round the throne; the encircling. The expositors observe the the elders and beasts: "and about the elders and the four beasts" — the redeemed church and the living creatures; the the elders; the the four beasts; the the church; the the creatures; the round. The witnesses press the fell on their faces: "fell before the throne on their faces" — the deepest reverence; the fell on their faces; the reverence; the adoration; the deepest; the bowed. The expositors draw the lesson on the worshipped God: "and worshipped God" — they worship the God who saves; the worshipped God; the worship; the the God; the who saves; the adore. The witnesses observe the no part yet rejoice: the angels have no part in salvation yet rejoice in it; the no part yet rejoice; the no part; the rejoice; the in it; the the salvation. The expositors note the united adoration: heaven's whole company bows in united adoration; the united adoration; the the whole company; the redeemed and angels; the bows; the the throne. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all the angels stood round about the throne and about the elders and the four beasts, the fell before the throne on their faces and worshipped God, and the angels joining the worship. The verse thus records the angels joining the heavenly worship — teaching that the innumerable angels join the redeemed in falling before the throne to worship God.
12:
"Saying, Amen: Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might, be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note the angels' sevenfold ascription of praise to God. Gill notes the angels say "Amen," approving and confirming what the great multitude had said in ascribing the glory of salvation to God and the Lamb; the angels, though they have no part in it, highly approve of it as right and just. The witnesses note John hearing the angels say, "Amen: Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might, be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen"; the angels' full and hearty doxology, sealing the praise of the redeemed — that the angels answer the redeemed multitude's song with their own great doxology, framed within a double "Amen": they first confirm the multitude's praise ("Amen"), then heap up a sevenfold ascription — "Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might" — all to be "unto our God for ever and ever," and seal it with a final "Amen"; the witnesses note the completeness of the praise (seven attributes, the number of perfection) and the heartiness of the angels, who, though not themselves redeemed, gladly own that all blessing and glory belong to God for ever; so that the worship of heaven swells in fullest praise to the saving God; Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might, be unto our God for ever and ever. The witnesses note Amen: Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might. The witnesses note be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen.

The witnesses note the angels' sevenfold doxology — blessing, glory, wisdom, thanksgiving, honour, power, might — to God for ever, framed by a double Amen; the fullest praise sealing the redeemed's song.

So the witnesses make the verse the angels' full doxology to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the angels confirm and crown the redeemed's praise with a sevenfold ascription of all glory to God for ever. The witnesses note the Amen: "Amen" — Gill notes the angels confirm the multitude's praise; the Amen; the confirm; the the multitude; the the praise; the approve. The expositors observe the sevenfold: "Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might" — seven attributes, the number of perfection; the sevenfold; the seven attributes; the perfection; the the praise; the full. The witnesses press the unto our God: "be unto our God for ever and ever" — all ascribed to God for ever; the unto our God; the for ever; the ascribed; the all; the eternal. The expositors draw the lesson on the double Amen: framed by a double "Amen"; the double Amen; the framed; the confirm; the seal; the Amen. The witnesses observe the angels' heartiness: the angels, though not redeemed, gladly own God's glory; the angels' heartiness; the not redeemed; the gladly; the God's glory; the own. The expositors note the worship swells: the worship of heaven swells in fullest praise; the worship swells; the heaven; the fullest praise; the the saving God; the adore. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Amen Blessing and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honour and power and might, the be unto our God for ever and ever Amen, and the angels' doxology. The verse thus records the angels' full doxology to God — teaching that the angels confirm and crown the redeemed's praise with all glory to God for ever.
13:
"And one of the elders answered, saying unto me, What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they?" The witnesses note one of the elders questioning John about the white-robed multitude. Gill notes this elder was not the Apostle Peter, nor Pope Sylvester, as some have fancied; he was one of the four and twenty elders, who puts the question to draw out and instruct John. The witnesses note John recording that one of the elders asked him who the white-robed company were and whence they came; the elder's question opening the way to reveal who the redeemed multitude are — that one of the elders (representing the redeemed church) turns to John with a question: "What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they?"; the witnesses note the purpose of the question — it is not asked from the elder's ignorance, but, in the manner of teachers, to draw out John's attention and to lead to the gracious answer that follows; the question fixes the eye on the white-robed multitude and prompts the seer to consider who they are and how they came to such glory; so that the elder, by his question, opens the way for the revealing of the identity and history of the redeemed throng — the answer being given in the verses that follow; What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they? The witnesses note one of the elders answered, saying unto me. The witnesses note What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they?

The witnesses note one of the elders asking who the white-robed multitude are and whence they came — not from ignorance but to draw out John and open the way to the gracious answer that follows.

So the witnesses make the verse the elder's question about the redeemed. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elder, in the manner of teachers, asks to draw out John and lead to the revealing of who the redeemed are. The witnesses note the one of the elders: "one of the elders answered, saying unto me" — Gill notes one of the twenty-four elders; the one of the elders; the the twenty-four; the the church; the asks; the John. The expositors observe the not from ignorance: the question not from his own ignorance; the not from ignorance; the not his own; the the elder; the knows; the asks. The witnesses press the what are these: "What are these which are arrayed in white robes?" — who is the white-robed company; the what are these; the the white-robed; the the company; the who; the the multitude. The expositors draw the lesson on the whence came: "and whence came they?" — and how came they to such glory; the whence came; the how came; the to glory; the their history; the their origin. The witnesses observe the to draw out John: the question draws out John's attention; the to draw out John; the John's attention; the leads; the the answer; the teaches. The expositors note the opens the way: the elder opens the way for revealing the redeemed; the opens the way; the revealing; the the redeemed; the their identity; the the answer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the one of the elders answered saying unto me, the What are these which are arrayed in white robes and whence came they, and the elder's question. The verse thus records the elder's question about the redeemed — teaching that the elder asks to draw out John and lead to the revealing of who the redeemed are.
14:
"And I said unto him, Sir, thou knowest. And he said to me, These are they which came out of great tribulation, and have washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb." The witnesses note the elder's answer revealing the redeemed who washed their robes in the Lamb's blood. Gill notes John replies in a very humble, modest, and respectful manner, calling the elder "Sir," and owning that the elder knew, rather than himself. The witnesses note John saying, "Sir, thou knowest," and the elder answering that these came out of great tribulation and washed their robes white in the blood of the Lamb; the redeemed identified as those who came through tribulation and were cleansed by the Lamb's blood — that John, with humble reverence, owns "Sir, thou knowest" (deferring to the elder), and the elder gives the great answer: "These are they which came out of great tribulation, and have washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb"; the witnesses note the twofold description — first, their history of suffering, "came out of great tribulation" (passed through severe trials and persecutions on earth), and second, the ground of their purity and glory, "washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb"; the witnesses note the paradox and the truth — their robes are made "white" by being washed "in the blood" of Christ, for it is his atoning blood alone that cleanses from sin and clothes the soul in spotless righteousness; so that the redeemed owe their whiteness not to their own merit or their sufferings, but wholly to the cleansing blood of the Lamb; washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb. The witnesses note these are they which came out of great tribulation. The witnesses note have washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.

The witnesses note the redeemed identified — those who came out of great tribulation and washed their robes white in the Lamb's blood; their whiteness owed not to merit but wholly to Christ's cleansing blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the redeemed cleansed by the Lamb's blood. The lesson the expositors draw is that the redeemed came through tribulation and owe their purity wholly to the atoning blood of the Lamb. The witnesses note the thou knowest: "Sir, thou knowest" — Gill notes John's humble deference; the thou knowest; the humble; the deference; the the elder; the modest. The expositors observe the great tribulation: "came out of great tribulation" — passed through severe trials; the great tribulation; the severe trials; the persecutions; the on earth; the suffering. The witnesses press the washed their robes: "have washed their robes" — cleansed their robes; the washed their robes; the cleansed; the the robes; the purified; the made white. The expositors draw the lesson on the in the blood: "made them white in the blood of the Lamb" — white by the Lamb's blood; the in the blood; the the Lamb's blood; the made white; the cleansed; the atoning. The witnesses observe the the paradox: white by being washed in blood; the the paradox; the white by blood; the the truth; the cleanses; the spotless. The expositors note the not their merit: their whiteness owed not to merit but to the blood; the not their merit; the not sufferings; the the blood; the wholly; the the Lamb. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the these are they which came out of great tribulation, the have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb, and the redeemed cleansed. The verse thus records the redeemed cleansed by the Lamb's blood — teaching that the redeemed came through tribulation and owe their purity wholly to the atoning blood of the Lamb.
15:
"Therefore are they before the throne of God, and serve him day and night in his temple: and he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them." The witnesses note the blessed estate of the redeemed — serving God and dwelling with him. Gill notes they are before the throne not because of their tribulations or their robes, but because they were brought through them and washed by the grace and power of God. The witnesses note the elder saying that therefore they are before God's throne, serving him day and night in his temple, and he that sits on the throne shall dwell among them; the redeemed honoured to serve God unceasingly and to have God dwell with them — that the elder describes the blessedness of the cleansed: "therefore are they before the throne of God" (admitted to God's immediate presence, on the ground of the Lamb's blood, not their own works) "and serve him day and night in his temple" (their everlasting employment is the joyful, unwearied service and worship of God, "day and night," that is, continually, without ceasing or weariness) — "and he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them"; the witnesses note the crowning blessing — God himself "shall dwell among them," spreading his tabernacle over them, his immediate, abiding presence their eternal joy and shelter; so that the redeemed, cleansed by the blood, are brought to unending service of God and the everlasting dwelling of God with them; he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them. The witnesses note therefore are they before the throne of God, and serve him day and night in his temple. The witnesses note he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them.

The witnesses note the blessed estate of the redeemed — before God's throne, serving him unceasingly, with God himself dwelling among them; their cleansing issuing in unending service and God's abiding presence.

So the witnesses make the verse the redeemed serving God and God dwelling with them. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cleansed are admitted to God's presence, serve him unceasingly, and have God dwell among them. The witnesses note the before the throne: "therefore are they before the throne of God" — Gill notes admitted by the Lamb's blood; the before the throne; the admitted; the the Lamb's blood; the not works; the the presence. The expositors observe the serve day and night: "serve him day and night in his temple" — unceasing service and worship; the serve day and night; the unceasing; the the worship; the continually; the unwearied. The witnesses press the without weariness: continually, without ceasing or weariness; the without weariness; the continually; the no ceasing; the joyful; the service. The expositors draw the lesson on the dwell among them: "he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them" — God dwells among them; the dwell among them; the God; the his presence; the abiding; the tabernacle. The witnesses observe the crowning blessing: God's immediate presence their eternal joy; the crowning blessing; the God's presence; the eternal joy; the shelter; the abiding. The expositors note the unending service: cleansed by the blood, brought to unending service; the unending service; the cleansed; the the blood; the service; the the presence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the therefore are they before the throne of God and serve him day and night in his temple, the he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them, and the redeemed serving God. The verse thus records the redeemed serving God and God dwelling with them — teaching that the cleansed are admitted to God's presence, serve him unceasingly, and have God dwell among them.
16:
"They shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more; neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat." The witnesses note the freedom of the redeemed from all want and distress. Gill notes the words are taken out of Isaiah and will be true in a literal and corporeal sense — the saints are now often in hunger and thirst, then they shall be so no more; and also in a mystical sense, there will be no famine of the word. The witnesses note the elder declaring that they shall hunger no more, nor thirst, nor shall the sun or any heat smite them; the end of all want, hunger, thirst, and scorching for the glorified — that the elder sets out the negative blessedness of heaven, the removal of every want and pain: "they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more; neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat"; the witnesses note the comfort — all the wants and distresses that the saints knew on earth are forever ended; "hunger" and "thirst" (every craving and lack, of body and soul) shall be no more, and "the sun" and "heat" (the scorching trials, persecutions, and burning sorrows of this life) shall never again beat upon them; the witnesses note the borrowing from Isaiah's prophecy of the comfort of God's people; so that the glorified are wholly delivered from every form of want, pain, and affliction, in a state where no lack or burning trouble can ever reach them; they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more. The witnesses note they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more. The witnesses note neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat.

The witnesses note the redeemed freed from all want — hungering no more, thirsting no more, no sun or heat smiting them; every craving and scorching trial of this life forever ended.

So the witnesses make the verse the end of all want and distress for the glorified. The lesson the expositors draw is that the redeemed are forever freed from hunger, thirst, and every scorching trial. The witnesses note the hunger no more: "they shall hunger no more" — Gill notes the saints' hunger ended; the hunger no more; the the saints; the ended; the no lack; the no craving. The expositors observe the thirst no more: "neither thirst any more" — every thirst ended; the thirst no more; the every thirst; the ended; the no lack; the satisfied. The witnesses press the no sun nor heat: "neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat" — no scorching trial; the no sun nor heat; the scorching; the the trials; the persecutions; the the heat. The expositors draw the lesson on the from Isaiah: borrowed from Isaiah's prophecy of comfort; the from Isaiah; the the prophecy; the comfort; the God's people; the the promise. The witnesses observe the wants ended: all wants and distresses forever ended; the wants ended; the the distresses; the forever; the body and soul; the the lacks. The expositors note the no trouble reaches: no lack or burning trouble can ever reach them; the no trouble reaches; the no lack; the no burning; the the glorified; the delivered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they shall hunger no more neither thirst any more, the neither shall the sun light on them nor any heat, and the end of all want. The verse thus records the end of all want and distress — teaching that the redeemed are forever freed from hunger, thirst, and every scorching trial.
17:
"For the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters: and God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes." The witnesses note the Lamb shepherding the redeemed and God wiping away all tears. Gill notes the Lamb is in the midst of the throne, not before it as the multitude, nor round about it as the angels, but in the midst, being equal to him that sits upon it, on the same throne, having the same power. The witnesses note the elder declaring that the Lamb in the midst of the throne shall feed them and lead them to living fountains, and God shall wipe away all tears; the Lamb the Shepherd of the glorified, and God the comforter who ends all weeping — that the elder closes with the tenderest picture of heaven's blessedness: "the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters: and God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes"; the witnesses note the sweet reversal — the Lamb, who was slain, is now the Shepherd of his flock, "in the midst of the throne" (on the throne, equal with God), who shall "feed them" (as a shepherd his sheep) and "lead them unto living fountains of waters" (to the ever-flowing springs of life and joy, the fulness of God himself); and the crowning comfort: "God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes" (every sorrow ended, every grief comforted by God's own tender hand); so that the redeemed are forever fed, led, and comforted, all their tears wiped away by God, in unending blessedness; God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes. The witnesses note the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters. The witnesses note God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes.

The witnesses note the Lamb shepherding the redeemed — feeding them and leading them to living fountains — and God wiping away all tears; the slain Lamb now the Shepherd, every grief comforted by God's hand.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lamb shepherding and God comforting the redeemed. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Lamb feeds and leads the glorified to the springs of life, and God wipes away all their tears. The witnesses note the in the midst: "the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne" — Gill notes the Lamb on the throne, equal with God; the in the midst; the on the throne; the equal with God; the the same power; the the Lamb. The expositors observe the shall feed them: "shall feed them" — the slain Lamb now the Shepherd; the shall feed them; the the Shepherd; the the slain Lamb; the his flock; the feeds. The witnesses press the living fountains: "shall lead them unto living fountains of waters" — to the springs of life and joy; the living fountains; the the springs; the life; the joy; the the fulness. The expositors draw the lesson on the wipe away tears: "God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes" — every sorrow ended; the wipe away tears; the every sorrow; the ended; the comforted; the God's hand. The expositors note the forever comforted: the redeemed forever fed, led, and comforted; the forever comforted; the fed; the led; the blessedness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes, and the Lamb shepherding. The verse thus records the Lamb shepherding and God comforting the redeemed — teaching that the Lamb feeds and leads the glorified to the springs of life, and God wipes away all their tears.
